steps.action: Morris dancing is a form of English folk dance with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat.
steps.text: Morris dancing is a form of English folk dance with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat.

steps.action: It is based on rhythmic stepping and the executi
steps.text: It is based on rhythmic stepping and the execution of choreographed figures by a group of dancers in costume, usually wearing bell pads on their shins, their shoes or both.

steps.action: Sticks, swords, handkerchiefs, and a variety of
steps.text: Sticks, swords, handkerchiefs, and a variety of other implements may be wielded by the dancers.

steps.action: Morris dancing first appeared in England in the
steps.text: Morris dancing first appeared in England in the 15th century.

steps.action: Its earliest surviving mention dates to 1448 and
steps.text: Its earliest surviving mention dates to 1448 and records the payment of seven shillings to Morris dancers by the Goldsmiths' Company in London.

steps.action: The term Morris derives from the Spanish term mo
steps.text: The term Morris derives from the Spanish term morisco, although Morris dancing has no known historical connection to the Moors.

steps.action: Cecil Sharp, whose collecting of Morris dances p
steps.text: Cecil Sharp, whose collecting of Morris dances preserved many from extinction, suggested that it might have arisen from the dancers' blacking their faces as part of the necessary ritual disguise.

steps.action: The name is first recorded in English in the mid
steps.text: The name is first recorded in English in the mid-15th century as Morisk dance, moreys daunce, morisse daunce, i.

steps.action: The modern spelling Morris-dance first appeared
steps.text: The modern spelling Morris-dance first appeared in the 17th century.

steps.action: It is unclear how the dance came to be referred
steps.text: It is unclear how the dance came to be referred to as Moorish, "unless in reference to fantastic dancing or costumes", i.

steps.action: The English dance thus apparently arose as part
steps.text: The English dance thus apparently arose as part of a wider 15th-century European fashion for supposedly "Moorish" spectacle, which also left traces in Spanish and Italian folk dance.

steps.action: The dance became part of performances for the lo
steps.text: The dance became part of performances for the lower classes by the later 16th century.

steps.action: Henry VIII owned a gold salt cellar which depict
steps.text: Henry VIII owned a gold salt cellar which depicted a Morris dance with five dancers and a "tabrett".

steps.action: Almost nothing is known about the folk dances of
steps.text: Almost nothing is known about the folk dances of England before the mid-17th century.

steps.action: By the mid-17th century, the working peasantry t
steps.text: By the mid-17th century, the working peasantry took part in Morris dances, especially at Whitsun.

steps.action: Morris dancing continued in popularity until the
steps.text: Morris dancing continued in popularity until the Industrial Revolution and its accompanying social changes.

steps.action: By the late 19th century, and in the West Countr
steps.text: By the late 19th century, and in the West Country at least, Morris dancing was fast becoming more a local memory than an activity.

steps.action: Neal was looking for dances for her girls to per
steps.text: Neal was looking for dances for her girls to perform, and so the first revival performance was by young women in London.

steps.action: In the 1950s and especially the 1960s, there was
steps.text: In the 1950s and especially the 1960s, there was an explosion of new dance teams, some of them women's or mixed sides.

steps.action: At the time, there was often heated debate over
steps.text: At the time, there was often heated debate over the propriety and even legitimacy of women dancing the Morris, even though there is evidence as far back as the 16th century that there were female Morris dancers.

steps.action: All three bodies provide communication, advice,
steps.text: All three bodies provide communication, advice, insurance, instructionals (teaching sessions) and social and dancing opportunities to their members.

steps.action: While these traditions that had been recorded by
steps.text: While these traditions that had been recorded by Ceinwen Thomas and Catherine Margretta Thomas were not specifically Morris dances as recognised today, they were interpreted in new ways by Morris men.

steps.action: While one dance, Y Gaseg Eira would become a cen
steps.text: While one dance, Y Gaseg Eira would become a central part of the Welsh morris.

steps.action: Large regular events in this part of the country
steps.text: Large regular events in this part of the country include the Marlboro Morris Ale and Dancing America Rapper Tournament (the American offshoot of Dancing England Rapper Tournament).
